title: Flame tests and identifying ions
examTip: Flame test colours: Li‚Āļ = red, Na‚Āļ = yellow/orange, K‚Āļ = lilac, Ca¬≤‚Āļ = orange-red, Cu¬≤‚Āļ = green. Negative ion tests: CO‚āÉ¬≤‚ĀĽ with dilute acid (fizzes, CO‚āā produced); SO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ with BaCl‚āā solution (white precipitate); Cl‚ĀĽ/Br‚ĀĽ/I‚ĀĽ with AgNO‚āÉ solution (white/cream/yellow precipitate). State both the reagent AND the observation for full marks.
introTips: Flame tests identify the metal (positive) ion ‚ÄĒ the colour is caused by electrons being excited and releasing energy as visible light.
Learn the five flame test colours in order: Li red, Na yellow, K lilac, Ca orange-red, Cu green.
Negative ion tests need a specific reagent ‚ÄĒ you cannot identify CO‚āÉ¬≤‚ĀĽ with AgNO‚āÉ or SO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ with acid. Match reagent to ion.
In exam answers, always state the reagent AND the expected observation ‚ÄĒ 'add dilute acid; CO‚āā gas is produced, which turns limewater milky'.
Sodium is a common contamination in chemicals ‚ÄĒ its bright yellow can mask other flame test colours. This is worth knowing for evaluate questions.
intro: Imagine being handed an unknown white powder and asked what it is. Chemists face this challenge constantly ‚ÄĒ whether identifying minerals in a rock, checking the purity of a drug, or analysing pollution. Chemical analysis lets you figure out exactly which ions a substance contains, using a series of carefully chosen tests. Flame tests are one of the most satisfying because the result is instant and spectacular ‚ÄĒ a vivid colour that immediately tells you which metal ion is present. Combined with tests for negative ions, you can identify most ionic compounds from scratch.

keyPoints.heading: Flame tests ‚ÄĒ identifying metal ions
keyPoints.content:
Flame tests are used to identify metal (positive) ions. When a compound is placed in a flame, metal ions absorb energy, their electrons are excited to higher energy levels, and then emit light of a specific colour as the electrons return to ground state.

Method:
1. Clean a nichrome wire loop with dilute hydrochloric acid and hold it in the flame until no colour is produced.
2. Dip the clean loop into the test substance.
3. Hold it in the blue part of a Bunsen flame and observe the colour.

Flame test colours to memorise:
‚ÄĘ Li‚Āļ (lithium) ‚ÄĒ crimson / red
‚ÄĘ Na‚Āļ (sodium) ‚ÄĒ yellow / orange
‚ÄĘ K‚Āļ (potassium) ‚ÄĒ lilac
‚ÄĘ Ca¬≤‚Āļ (calcium) ‚ÄĒ orange-red
‚ÄĘ Cu¬≤‚Āļ (copper) ‚ÄĒ green
writeBullets: Flame test: metal ions excited by heat ‚Üí emit light of characteristic colour
Li‚Āļ = crimson/red
Na‚Āļ = yellow/orange
K‚Āļ = lilac
Ca¬≤‚Āļ = orange-red
Cu¬≤‚Āļ = green

keyTerms.def: A test used to identify metal ions by the colour they produce when heated in a Bunsen flame
A wire made of nickel-chromium alloy, used in flame tests because it does not produce its own colour in a flame

keyPoints.memTip: Little Nasty Kids Can't Guess: Li=red, Na=yellow, K=lilac, Ca=orange-red, Cu=green. Or think: copper gives GREEN like a green copper roof.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often confuse Ca (orange-red) and Na (yellow/orange). Stress that sodium is a brighter, pure yellow and is the most striking. Calcium is more of a brick-orange.

keyPoints.heading: Testing for carbonate ions (CO‚āÉ¬≤‚ĀĽ)
keyPoints.content:
To test for carbonate ions, you add dilute hydrochloric acid (or any dilute acid) to the substance.

Observation if CO‚āÉ¬≤‚ĀĽ is present: Effervescence (fizzing/bubbling) is produced. The gas produced is carbon dioxide (CO‚āā).

Confirming CO‚āā: Bubble the gas through limewater (calcium hydroxide solution). If CO‚āā is present, the limewater turns milky / cloudy (a white precipitate of calcium carbonate forms).

Equation: Na‚āāCO‚āÉ + 2HCl ‚Üí 2NaCl + H‚āāO + CO‚āā

The ionic equation: CO‚āÉ¬≤‚ĀĽ + 2H‚Āļ ‚Üí H‚āāO + CO‚āā
writeBullets: Test for CO‚āÉ¬≤‚ĀĽ: add dilute acid
Observation: fizzing/effervescence ‚ÄĒ CO‚āā gas produced
Confirm CO‚āā: bubble through limewater ‚ÄĒ turns milky/cloudy
Equation: CO‚āÉ¬≤‚ĀĽ + 2H‚Āļ ‚Üí H‚āāO + CO‚āā

keyTerms.def: A negative ion containing carbon and oxygen; identified by fizzing with dilute acid to produce CO‚āā
Calcium hydroxide solution; turns milky/cloudy in the presence of CO‚āā, confirming carbonate ions

keyPoints.memTip: Carbonate + acid = fizz. The bubble is CO‚āā. Check by bubbling through limewater ‚ÄĒ milky means CO‚āā confirmed.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students must give both the reagent AND the observation for full marks. 'Add dilute acid' alone is not enough ‚ÄĒ they must say 'fizzing/effervescence is seen' and ideally confirm with limewater.

keyPoints.heading: Testing for sulfate ions (SO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ) and halide ions (Cl‚ĀĽ, Br‚ĀĽ, I‚ĀĽ)
keyPoints.content:
Testing for sulfate ions (SO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ):
Add barium chloride (BaCl‚āā) solution (acidified with dilute HCl).
Observation if SO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ present: a white precipitate of barium sulfate (BaSO‚āĄ) forms immediately. It is insoluble in dilute acid (which rules out other precipitates).
Equation: Ba¬≤‚Āļ + SO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ ‚Üí BaSO‚āĄ (s)

Testing for halide ions (Cl‚ĀĽ, Br‚ĀĽ, I‚ĀĽ):
Add silver nitrate solution (AgNO‚āÉ) (acidified with dilute HNO‚āÉ).
‚ÄĘ Cl‚ĀĽ: white precipitate (AgCl)
‚ÄĘ Br‚ĀĽ: cream/pale yellow precipitate (AgBr)
‚ÄĘ I‚ĀĽ: yellow precipitate (AgI)

Acidify with dilute nitric acid (not HCl or H‚āāSO‚āĄ, which would introduce Cl‚ĀĽ or SO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ and interfere with the test).
writeBullets: SO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ test: add BaCl‚āā solution (acidified) ‚Üí white precipitate of BaSO‚āĄ
BaSO‚āĄ is insoluble in dilute acid (this rules out false positives)
Halide test: add AgNO‚āÉ solution (acidified with HNO‚āÉ)
Cl‚ĀĽ: white precipitate (AgCl)
Br‚ĀĽ: cream precipitate (AgBr)
I‚ĀĽ: yellow precipitate (AgI)

keyTerms.def: A negative ion identified by adding BaCl‚āā solution ‚ÄĒ gives a white precipitate of BaSO‚āĄ insoluble in dilute acid
Reagent used to test for halide ions; produces white (Cl‚ĀĽ), cream (Br‚ĀĽ), or yellow (I‚ĀĽ) precipitates
An insoluble solid formed when two solutions react and produce an insoluble product

keyPoints.memTip: Sulfate: BaCl‚āā ‚Üí WHITE precipitate (BaSO‚āĄ). Halides: AgNO‚āÉ ‚Üí White (Cl), Cream (Br), Yellow (I). Go lighter to darker: Cl is lightest, I is deepest yellow.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students sometimes use HCl to acidify silver nitrate tests ‚ÄĒ this introduces Cl‚ĀĽ ions and would give a white precipitate even without Cl‚ĀĽ in the unknown. Stress: use HNO‚āÉ only to acidify the halide test.

keyPoints.heading: Using tests in combination ‚ÄĒ identifying an unknown ionic compound
keyPoints.content:
In practice (and in exam questions), you combine tests to identify a complete ionic compound. Work through methodically:

1. Do a flame test ‚Üí identify the positive (metal) ion.
2. Test for CO‚āÉ¬≤‚ĀĽ with dilute acid (fizzing?).
3. Test for SO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ with acidified BaCl‚āā (white precipitate?).
4. Test for halide with acidified AgNO‚āÉ (white/cream/yellow precipitate?).

Example: Unknown compound gives a yellow flame (‚Üí Na‚Āļ) and a white precipitate with acidified AgNO‚āÉ (‚Üí Cl‚ĀĽ). Compound = sodium chloride (NaCl).

Always state: (a) what you did, (b) what you observed, (c) what it tells you ‚ÄĒ for every test.
writeBullets: Step 1: flame test ‚Üí identify metal ion
Step 2: dilute acid ‚Üí CO‚āÉ¬≤‚ĀĽ if fizzing (confirm with limewater)
Step 3: acidified BaCl‚āā ‚Üí SO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ if white precipitate
Step 4: acidified AgNO‚āÉ ‚Üí Cl‚ĀĽ (white), Br‚ĀĽ (cream), I‚ĀĽ (yellow)
State: reagent added, observation, conclusion ‚ÄĒ for full marks

keyTerms.def: Chemical analysis that identifies which substances are present (not how much) ‚ÄĒ flame tests and ion precipitation tests are examples

keyPoints.memTip: Work through tests in order: fire first (flame test), then acid (carbonate), then BaCl‚āā (sulfate), then AgNO‚āÉ (halide). Four steps, four answers.
keyPoints.tutor_note: In longer questions, students earn marks for each correct (reagent, observation) pair. Practise writing these as two linked sentences for every test described.

tables.title: Negative ion tests ‚ÄĒ reagents and observations
rows: Effervescence (fizzing); CO‚āā produced ‚ÄĒ turns limewater milky
White precipitate of BaSO‚āĄ (insoluble in dilute acid)

0.question: A compound gives a lilac flame in a flame test. Which ion is present?
0.correctFeedback: Correct! A lilac flame indicates potassium ions (K‚Āļ). Yellow = Na‚Āļ, red = Li‚Āļ, orange-red = Ca¬≤‚Āļ, green = Cu¬≤‚Āļ.
0.wrongFeedback: Lilac flame = K‚Āļ (potassium). Sodium is yellow/orange, lithium is red, calcium is orange-red, and copper is green.

1.question: A student adds silver nitrate solution (acidified with HNO‚āÉ) to an unknown solution and gets a cream precipitate. Which ion is present?
1.correctFeedback: Correct! AgNO‚āÉ gives: white = Cl‚ĀĽ, cream = Br‚ĀĽ, yellow = I‚ĀĽ. Cream precipitate (AgBr) means bromide ions are present.
1.wrongFeedback: With AgNO‚āÉ (acidified): white precipitate = Cl‚ĀĽ (AgCl), cream precipitate = Br‚ĀĽ (AgBr), yellow precipitate = I‚ĀĽ (AgI). Cream means bromide (Br‚ĀĽ).

3.question: What would you observe when testing a solution containing SO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ ions with acidified BaCl‚āā solution?
3.correctFeedback: Spot on! Sulfate ions react with Ba¬≤‚Āļ to form BaSO‚āĄ ‚ÄĒ a white precipitate that is insoluble in dilute acid. Ba¬≤‚Āļ + SO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ ‚Üí BaSO‚āĄ(s).
3.wrongFeedback: Sulfate ions (SO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ) + barium chloride solution ‚Üí white precipitate of barium sulfate (BaSO‚āĄ). Effervescence means carbonate (CO‚āÉ¬≤‚ĀĽ), not sulfate.

commonMistakes: Confusing sodium (yellow) with calcium (orange-red) in flame tests ‚ÄĒ sodium is a bright, pure yellow; calcium has a more orange-red brick colour.
Using the wrong reagent: adding AgNO‚āÉ to test for sulfate (it won't work) or BaCl‚āā to test for halides. Each ion has its specific reagent.
Acidifying the silver nitrate test with HCl instead of HNO‚āÉ ‚ÄĒ adding HCl introduces chloride ions, which would give a false white precipitate.
Not describing the observation in enough detail ‚ÄĒ 'a precipitate forms' is not enough; say 'a white precipitate forms' or 'effervescence is seen'.
Confusing the bromine water test (for alkenes) with the flame test or ion tests ‚ÄĒ these are entirely separate tests for different purposes.
examTips: For every ion test, write two things: (1) the reagent you add, and (2) the exact observation if the ion is present. Two marks = two things.
When describing the carbonate test, always include the confirmatory test: bubble the gas through limewater and state that it turns milky.
Remember to acidify BaCl‚āā with dilute HCl and AgNO‚āÉ with dilute HNO‚āÉ ‚ÄĒ using the wrong acid in a practical question will cost marks.

If a question asks you to identify a compound, combine your results: 'The yellow flame shows Na‚Āļ ions are present. The white precipitate with AgNO‚āÉ shows Cl‚ĀĽ ions are present. Therefore the compound is sodium chloride.'
revisionCardBullets: Li‚Āļ = red, Na‚Āļ = yellow, K‚Āļ = lilac, Ca¬≤‚Āļ = orange-red, Cu¬≤‚Āļ = green
CO‚āÉ¬≤‚ĀĽ: dilute acid ‚Üí fizzing (CO‚āā); limewater turns milky to confirm
SO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ: BaCl‚āā solution (acidified with HCl) ‚Üí white precipitate (BaSO‚āĄ)
Cl‚ĀĽ: AgNO‚āÉ (acidified with HNO‚āÉ) ‚Üí white precipitate (AgCl)
Br‚ĀĽ: AgNO‚āÉ (acidified with HNO‚āÉ) ‚Üí cream precipitate (AgBr)
I‚ĀĽ: AgNO‚āÉ (acidified with HNO‚āÉ) ‚Üí yellow precipitate (AgI)
Acidify AgNO‚āÉ test with HNO‚āÉ NOT HCl
Always state: reagent added + exact observation for full marks
Precipitate = insoluble solid formed in solution
